reasoning: The resolution criteria require the 30-day explosion/remote violence event count in Lebanon to exceed 9,133 events (10 times the baseline of 1 + 912.333). This baseline of ~912 events per month represents an entire period of intense warfare (April 2024–April 2025) which included the historical peak of the Israel-Hezbollah war and the Israeli ground invasion. For a single 30-day period to exceed 9,133 events, Lebanon would have to witness over 300 distinct explosion/remote violence events every single day for a month. Given ACLED's spatial and temporal aggregation methodology (where multiple strikes on the same location on the same day by the same actor are coded as a single event), achieving over 300 distinct events per day in a country the size of Lebanon is virtually physically and methodologically impossible. Furthermore, a ceasefire is currently in effect, meaning the current baseline is very low. Even in the unlikely event of a massive resumption of full-scale war, the event counts would peak closer to ~2,000–3,000 per month, falling far short of the 9,133 threshold. Therefore, the probability of hitting this threshold is at the absolute minimum for the near-term dates and very slightly higher for later dates solely to reflect the mathematical decay of forecast certainty over time.
